title: Binary search trees - order that searches itself
author: Prodipta Acharjee
concept: A binary search tree keeps an ordering rule - everything left of a node is smaller, everything right is larger - so searching follows one path down, in about log n steps when it's balanced.
explanation: Take a binary tree and add one rule: for every node, the whole left side is smaller and the whole right side is bigger. Now searching is just binary search wearing a tree: at each node, go left if your target is smaller, right if it's bigger. You touch one node per level, so a balanced tree finds anything in about log n steps. The warning: if you insert already-sorted data, it can degrade into a single long line - and then search slumps back to O(n). Balance is the whole game.
code_example:
class Node:
    def __init__(self, v):
        self.v = v
        self.left = None
        self.right = None

root = Node(8)
root.left = Node(3)
root.right = Node(10)
# searching for 3: is 3 < 8? go left.
node = root.left if 3 < root.v else root.right
print(node.v)

exercises.question: What does this print?
hints: 3 < 8 is true, so it goes left.

exercises.question: In a binary search tree, everything to a node's left is...
hints: That ordering rule is the whole point.
Left is the smaller side, right is larger.

exercises.question: Searching a balanced binary search tree is...
hints: You drop one level per step, halving the rest.
